title: A design systems role in web accessibility
content: ## How can a design system contribute to accessibility in a complex ecosystem of web apps? SEB develops and maintains hundreds of web apps. Perhaps even thousands, if you count various internal tools. This is developed and maintained by hundreds of developers across many, many teams. “But wait a minute,” you may be thinking now. “I’ve only used internetbanken, that’s one app??” Well, not quite. Internetbanken consists of many different parts. Pretty much every page is a separate app, and sometimes there are even multiple apps on one page. These apps are called micro-frontends, or MFEs for short. This is an agile architectural approach that enables software development at scale and makes it possible to distribute the work among many different teams. This does leave a lot of room for variation however. Varying degrees of knowledge, different tastes. Some developers have eyes for visual design, for others it’s all about function. For some, accessibility is front and center, for others, more of an afterthough. This is all fine – we’re all different, and that’s ok. But when the goal is to deliver a cohesive and accessible customer experience, we need to find a common baseline. That’s the role of the design system! ## The tech stack To understand a bit better where the design system fits into all of this, consider the front-end tech stack. It can be roughly illustrated as a pyramid with three segments. At the base, we have the web platform itself, or more concretely, the browser. Then, on the middle layer, there is typically some kind of front-end framework. At SEB, most apps use either Angular or React. And finally, at the top of the pyramid sits the app code. Business logic and UI code specific to this app. This is where the aforementioned variation comes in, both good and bad. This is also where a11y mistakes happen. Missing or incorrect semantics, unlabelled elements, or even bad use of ARIA, all happen in that top segment. This is where the design system enters the picture! By wedging itself between the framework and app code layers, the design system, with it's component library, can eliminate the need for individual app teams to write the nitty-gritty UI code where many of those a11y mistakes often happen. And of course, it also brings with it the visual cohesion for all those elements! ## Greens approach So what actually goes into this design system layer? At the core of the design system lies our component library, aptly named Green Core. This is a library of framework-agnostic web components. Components are accessible by default and respects user preferences. We also provide a component-based declarative layout system, paired with a design token setup with accessible contrasts and built-in responsive features. In addition to these component library features, we also provide extensive guidelines and documentation on this website and in Storybook. We have a built-in MCP for coding agents, so they can consume this documentation easily. This improves accuracy and reduces token spend. And finally we provide bookable coaching sessions, where teams can get hands-on help from the design system team in real-time. ## Components as encapsulated semantic entities In Green Core, we think of each component as a discrete encapsulated semantic entity. That’s just a fancy way of saying that each component has design, behavior and accessibility built-in out of the box. This encapsulation is sometimes as simple as an inner `
